speaker: interviewer
text: Tape three. Jacob, there was something happened with the partisans that you wanted to tell us about, when you were there?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was one time I was in one of the villages. The name of the village was Huta Werchobuzka. And I was-- it was in the late afternoon. I was in the house with the people there. Three partisans, like-- they were-- came in. Like, three gentiles came in. They were dressed like plain, ordinary people. They asked the owner there, of the house-- the owner, a friend there. And they were, like, having a drink. They wanted to join the partisans. And as soon as they started to talk, I noticed they had a German accent. They didn't know that. They didn't even know I was Jewish. Anyways, they had drinks and food. Anyways, they left, those three of them. So I told the people, don't believe a word what these people say. Because they are not-- they are not Russians. They are not Ukrainians. They are not the partisans. They are Germans.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he asked me, what do you-- how do you know? I said, they have an accent, like a German accent. They didn't notice that.

speaker: interviewee
text: Anyways, they left. I slept there. About 6 o'clock in the morning-- and 7 o'clock in the winter, I looked out a window. The whole village was a long village, like, a one street, like young street on each side. From every side, there were, like, the German army, with the-- all in white with skis and machine guns. The whole village was surrounded, the whole village. They took out every able-bodied-- like, the people there. And these three of them were dressed German officers, with uniforms and everything. But they had, like, ski, ski, like masks and coat, everything in white. They took them all away. I've never seen them again, those people.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you get out of this?

words.text: did you get out of this?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was a kid. They didn't bother me. They only took the-- the people, the older ones. And they took everything else. They took the-- like, the sacks of corn, potatoes, the cows. They, they cleaned out the complete, complete village. They cleaned them out. Anyway, I left right after. But when I-- when I saw them, I turned around, like, they shouldn't see me. Because I was in the house at the time. But all three of them were, like, high-ranking officers. They weren't plain soldiers.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you part of the partisans at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Right after. Right after, when I got into the woods, right after that, I joined them. Right after.

speaker: interviewer
text: So many-- so many incidents. It's, it's, it's, it's hard. It's very hard to, you know-- to describe. I could write a book like this. Are there any other incidents you want to talk about?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, to tell-- to tell you the truth, there, there's too many. I don't know which one to think of. But this one particular I remember, like, like now. So I just wanted to mention it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Then we can go ahead now, and we can talk about your getting out of the army.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I got out in the army in--

speaker: interviewer
text: It was about '46?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, in '46, I got out the army in Dzierzoniow. It was-- it was me. And also one of the sergeants, I was in there, also. We got out at the same time. He knew already I was Jewish. And I-- And we-- I stayed with him.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the sergeant's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Simon Judenstein. He had a wife and two kids in Russia. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And as soon as we got out of the army, we lived in Dzierzoniow. And then the-- the, the immigrants started to come back from Russia. So his wife and the kids came back to Cieszyn. And I went with him. And we picked them up. And I lived with them till, till I left Poland, actually. But I was-- lived there. But I also-- I joined the, the kibbutz. And I almost went to Israel from there.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did that happen? Can you tell us about that?

speaker: interviewee
text: When I joined the kibbutz, you know--

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was the kibbutz?

speaker: interviewee
text: The kibbutz was in that town, Reichenbach. Dzierzoniow, it's called now. There were quite a few kibbutzim. There were [INAUDIBLE], and this one here in the picture that I have, that was Ichud. And those kibbutzim, they used to leave sometimes 4 o'clock in the morning. They used to cross over the Czech border, into Austria, go to Palestine. Matter of fact, one of my friends, his name was [? Gennick. ?] Somebody told me, like, he went to the Exodus. He died on the Exodus. He was sick.

speaker: interviewer
text: The kibbutz that you went to, approximately how many were there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, quite a-- quite a-- quite a-- quite a-- quite a-- a, a lot. A lot of kids were there, in the kibbutz. But I, I didn't stay in the kibbutz. I lived with them. And I was a member in the kibbutz. And I was going to go, leave for Israel. But then I met a friend of mine, like, my two-year-older brother. He says, you know, you have a brother in Gliwice, right the day before they left like, to cross the border into Austria. So I stayed.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which brother was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: Abie. He was the oldest one, like, a half brother. He was-- and the two-year-older brother was liquidated, like, in the-- in camp. They liquidated most of the camps. I had two brothers, in the [INAUDIBLE], Lemberg, Lviv, which is now Lviv. There was no escape from that camp. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] So from the whole family, it was me, the youngest, and the oldest.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, so you stayed. And you-- did you get to meet your-- with your brother?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I--

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did that take?

speaker: interviewee
text: It didn't take very long. It didn't take very long. Matter of fact, he came to that town, I think, a couple of days. And we met at a soccer match. And then I went there, back, it was in the Gliwice. I went back with him. And from there, he already filled out-- like, he knew the family in New York. And we were going to send affidavit, go to New York. So I figured, you know, I'll go with him.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we went to Paris in 1947. I stayed there for about a year. We couldn't go no place. So there was a Jewish-American, the Joint committee. Anyway, I went up and I wanted to go to Israel. Because I couldn't go no place. I didn't want to stay in Paris. So one of the leaders says, where were you, this and that? So I showed him the document. So he says to me, I think maybe you had enough. How would you like to go with the orphans to United States, Canada, here and there?

speaker: interviewee
text: To get out of-- I didn't want to stay in Paris. I said, OK. So I came to Canada in 1948.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you get food and clothing? And how did you support yourself throughout this time? Before that?

speaker: interviewee
text: OK, After I got out of the Army, we lived in Dzierzoniow. So from Dzierzoniow, we used to buy, like, clothes we used to take to Warsaw. And from Warsaw, we knew the owner from the Wedel factories. They make chocolates. And we used to bring back chocolates to Dzierzoniow. And we used to buy and sell. This is how people used to make a living in those days.

speaker: interviewer
text: So that's how you supported yourself?

speaker: interviewee
text: The only-- the only thing is, while I was in Poland-- and every time I used to go to Warsaw-- I used to wear my uniform. Because you couldn't get on a train. So I used to go with the officers. And he also wore his uniform.

speaker: interviewer
text: We used to-- we used to go in with the officers. They, they had a special, like, car, soldiers, officers. The rest? You couldn't get on a train. One time, from Warsaw to Katowice, and I-- I was on top of the roof. Almost got killed. Do you want to tell us about that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, there was no place. You couldn't get in on the trains. So people hang on. They hang on between the cars, and the rails, and the roof. Whenever there's a little bit of room, that's where they got on and hold on. But then you get through those tunnels. So I felt it, you know, like a-- like another inch, could have gotten killed. And most people, like, right after the war, this was how they traveled. But the army always used to have the, you know, the wagons. I used to travel with-- always in my uniform. And I used to get the best seats. They didn't ask. They didn't ask-- soldiers, they never asked any questions. Never.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have your medals then?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes, I had my medals then.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you wear them when you were traveling as well?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I only wore them on occasions, like, like, you know, special occasions. I-- like, you know, they used to have marches, other things. I used to put them on. Otherwise, I never bothered with them.

speaker: interviewer
text: You didn't stay with your brother, after, after the war? Once you found him?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I stayed with him, after that, for, for-- not for long. When he already made out the papers, we went to Paris, France.

speaker: interviewer
text: And, and what happened to your brother?

speaker: interviewee
text: My brother stayed in Paris. He couldn't go. When I came here, I think it was a few years later, two or three years, I filled out affidavit papers. And he came here.

speaker: interviewer
text: He was too old to be an orphan?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was too old to be an orphan, yeah. And from here, he went to New York and lived in New York and got married there.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened that you weren't able to get to New York?

speaker: interviewee
text: I stayed here.

speaker: interviewer
text: No. Before, you said you had family in New York.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, but they were waiting for the Polish quota. And who knows how long? So that's what happened. We stayed in Paris. I came here. And after I was here for a while, I made out papers for him. He came here. He stayed here, I think, for a couple of years. And then he went to New York.

speaker: interviewer
text: You stayed with--

speaker: interviewee
text: I stayed with a family here. The Jewish Congress brought me over here, like, from-- like from Paris, with the orphans. And I came here with the orphans in '48.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who did you stay with?

speaker: interviewee
text: I stayed with a family, [? Kochberg. ?] Right here, on Beatrice Street, Beatrice and College. I think I stayed with them for about six years. They treated me like one of their own. Till I got married.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did you do when you came here?

speaker: interviewee
text: When I came here, I went to school for two years. Went to grade eight, Clinton Street school. I passed from grade eight. I went to Central Tech for one year. And right before the end of the year, I quit because somebody offered me a job as a cutter. And a cutter, in those days, was supposed to be good. Blue collars, it was good, those days. I couldn't-- I actually wanted to go into communications. But my language wasn't too good. So I got into this, to this day.

speaker: interviewer
text: That was cutting dresses and, and, and-- like, in a tailor shop, in the garment district?

speaker: interviewee
text: I became a cutter. One, one place, I stayed for about 17 years. And this place, I'm also, I think, about 20 years. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But now it's a little different.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you met your wife here?

speaker: interviewee
text: I met my wife here, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your wife's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Libby.

speaker: interviewer
text: Libby?

speaker: interviewee
text: Libby, her maiden name?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes, please.

speaker: interviewee
text: Libby Goldberg.

speaker: interviewer
text: We want to thank you ever so much for sharing your story with us. But before we close off, is there something you would like to say to the future generations?

speaker: interviewee
text: I hope the future generation will learn something from these stories. And the-- and the only thing is they should-- they should do something about it, not to sit and wait. If they see anything, fight. Don't sit. This is what I'd like to see for the-- for the-- for the Jewish and, and any new generation.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: I'd like you to meet-- this is my wife, my lovely wife, Libby Gang.

speaker: interviewer
text: Will you tell us about these pictures?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, these pictures were taken, like, in the army, but by another soldier. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] This picture on the-- on the right was, like, the combat uniform. And the other one is the Sunday uniform. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Whenever we used to have-- it's called the defilade, like, you know, when you march from the-- like, parading. So we used to wear the, the Sunday uniform with the medals. You know? To look nice. And there was a story about you and a long coat.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK, the story is like this. When we came to Czesterchowa, when I-- when I-- when I met all these-- you know, like, the kibbutz and the Jewish people, it was run-- guy by Diego. So I had a long coat, like the army, long coat. And I didn't want a long coat. So I met a Jewish tailor there. His name is [INAUDIBLE]. And he shortened it for me. Like, I met a lot of friends there. And then about-- that was in 1946.

speaker: interviewer
text: Then I went to Seneca College here. I think it was, about three, four years ago. It must have been in, in 1990. So we went to a concert in Seneca College. I was there with my wife. And I said, to my wife, you see this man here? I know him. I wonder if he remembers me.

speaker: interviewee
text: And when I went over to him, and we started to talk, we, we both-- we both almost cried. I saw tears. He couldn't-- I couldn't believe it. He couldn't believe it either. I forgot his name. I didn't know his name. But he lives right here in, in in Toronto. I think he--

speaker: interviewer
text: This was-- this was the tailor?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was the tailor. I think he's the president of the [INAUDIBLE] Society now.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell us about this?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, this was taken, really, I think, on German soil. These are-- these are-- these are, like, the first two. They are doctors. And the third one's a doctor. And this is one of the sergeants that was in the-- in the fifth battalion, right at, at the end of the war, almost.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where are you?

speaker: interviewee
text: And I'm right beside them, on the right-hand side in front.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the sergeant's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: The sergeant's name is Simon Shimmel-- like Simon Judenstein.

speaker: interviewer
text: And that's the gentleman you stayed with after the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. They, they went-- they all went to Israel, him and his family.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he's on the right-hand side?

speaker: interviewee
text: He's on the right-hand side.

speaker: interviewer
text: Far right-hand side, behind you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Right, right. I think his son-- his son was in the army. And the girl was in the army. And he was killed, the son, in the early army.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Can you tell us about this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was also taken on German soil. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It's like the soldiers, most of them had cameras. And one of them took the picture of us. This is also the sergeant I'm with. I think, I believe, that this is taken near Cottbus, in Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is after the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Can you tell us about this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, this picture was taken when I joined the-- a kibbutz in Poland, in Dzierzoniow. And I think it was Ichud. It used to be [INAUDIBLE]. It's a-- Ichud, we used to play soccer with each other. But this one was Ichud. It was also in the 1940s, right after I got out of the Army. When I joined the kibbutz, they gave us uniforms. I figured I might as well put the medals on.

speaker: interviewer
text: So-- and these medals, you want to tell us?

speaker: interviewee
text: These medals are what-- one of them what I earned. The other one are for-- from campaigns that you go through.

speaker: interviewer
text: While you were in the army?

speaker: interviewee
text: While I was in the army, yes. And here is all the papers, like the-- This is the order for liberating the Polish soil that was taken by the Germans, I think, in 1917 and, like, before. That-- This is the order that I received. This is for liberating the land that used to belong to Poland. It's called Prussia, which is now Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Can you tell us-- this is describing?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is describing the order that I got, what for it is, for the-- for liberating, like, the land that belonged to Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this is the paper that describes that?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is the paper that describes the order.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, you want to tell us about this?

speaker: interviewee
text: This one here is for the-- only for bravery, what-- what you did.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is a medal that you earned?

speaker: interviewee
text: This, this is the medal that a person has to earn. Not everybody gets these.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you know what, what you earned this for?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I was a scout in the communications. And I did a lot of volunteer work, very dangerous work that-- nobody wanted to do it.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Can you tell us?

speaker: interviewee
text: This one here, I-- I, I earned it, I would say, on the battlefield. Because I was always a volunteer. You know, the scouts, communications.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this is the other side of the one that we just saw?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is the other side of what you saw.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, can you tell us about the book that you received when you were being discharged from the army?

speaker: interviewee
text: When I-- when I got discharged, this is what they gave me, the book with the-- with the whole record.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, can you tell us what's-- and what are we looking at now?

speaker: interviewee
text: Right now, we're looking at the-- when I got into the army.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which was?

speaker: interviewee
text: Which was in [INAUDIBLE]. And [INAUDIBLE], like, the next page is the campaigns.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, well, we'll go on to that. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Can you tell us about this page now?

speaker: interviewee
text: This page is for the medals that I received for the campaigns that-- what-- that I went through, from Poland to, to Berlin. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] All the medals I have for, for what-- for what I went through there.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is the continuation of the campaign for the last page that you did.

speaker: interviewer
text: Good. OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my army book, the records of right through the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the name on that army book?

speaker: interviewee
text: The name is what-- that's what I used as-- as a gentile, Sandel Janek. That was my name. And that's what everybody knew right till the end of the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then you were known as?

speaker: interviewee
text: Now I'm known as Jacob Gang. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] That, that was in the kibbutz, in Dzierzoniow, for 1946. And most of-- most of them went to Israel. And I almost went, too. I'm sorry. And I'm sorry [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewer
text: And you, is this you, here?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I think that's me. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And the name of the kibbutz?

speaker: interviewee
text: I, I don't know the, the name. I know it was a Ichud. But the name of it, I don't remember. There was a [INAUDIBLE] a year, Ichud-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] different, different names. Every kibbutz had a different name. But I don't know exactly the name of this one.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Can you tell us about this?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, this was already 1946, and everything, you know, was OK. We were looking for relatives and friends. And we, we started to make a new life for ourselves.

speaker: interviewer
text: And which one are you in this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm the second one from the left.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you're with friends?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm with friends, yes.
